titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Duffin to Celia Duffin, 19 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Maria Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.
Celia Duffin was a VAD with the 3rd London General Hospital in Wandsworth which opened in 1914. She was an accomplished poet, publishing, amongst other volumes, a book of poetry with her sister Ruth.
This is a letter from mother to daughter in which she gives all the news from home. She has news of a death, an engagement and a military promotion and she tells of various people she has met or visited recently and of illnesses. She writes about a bureau she has recently acquired.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D2109/10/1/C DUNOWEN,
BELFAST. March 19'1916. My dearest Celia. This week is a
blank as to anything doing
and, I suppose, you will
soon get a sight of Dorothy
who crossed by on
Friday night, en route for
the Garretts' as Hilda had
been in contact with Measles
and she thought it better
to take Andrews first, in
case of complications. Ruth came yesterday, but
may have to join Marjorie
tomorrow as Sam & Iva go and
Charlie is at Aldershot.
She is lunching with Dora today.
I saw poor old Mrs Riddel
on Monday. a perfect wreck.
So much worse. than on the
day of the funeral. I really
could not leave them if I
were Marjorie. I think she
has made no plans yet, but
may, after she has seen Charlie.
He is wanted over. for some
arrangements. Aunt Margaret
has left him the big car and
I suppose there are other things.
However, these is no need to
move anything at present. Poor Mrs Leviney had an
operation yesterday for a
tumour and appendicitis.
I am anxious to hear about
her. Such an unfortunate time.
We have had Mrs Garrett
Campbell and Mrs Poliyard
and reveal much letters.
A very nice one from Mayor
Boylan Smith. I am glad you had your day
at Eastbourne. Olive would be
glad to see you. My bureau has arrived. It is not
an "antique" as I had fancied
but is quite nice. We have
put the sofa into the hollow
of the piano, at Smyth's suggestion
and. I think it is a great
improvement. I am glad the verses arrived
safely. I will pay for them
the next time I am that
way. They were to fund
out postage. I sent you a
currant loaf, but, in future
I think a postal order to buy
one will be better, the postage
mounts up beyond its worth.
Shall I send some more soup
and what is the best? Yesterday. I did a little "forking
up" in the garden. but today
it is raining again. Old Mrs "P" Davidson is dead
and was quite queer for
some time before. I have heard
nothing of Willie Ewing, but
must go and see Minnie
this week, now that she is
alone. Aunt Charlotte is still in
Craig's hands about her ears
and Ruth still complains
of "ringing" while my ears
are much the same alas! Why do complaints seem to
DUNOWEN,
BELFAST. come in batches? Peter is very good. except when
he goes fautee. He keeps me
very nervous about the kittens
One of them is a darling. Elsie Kingan's engagement
is announced and Captain
Dane is now a Brigadier General!
I had tea with Mrs Lindan
on Sunday; we are very great
now. I hear Effie is at
Hoylalne with a Miss Rogers
and a nurse and writing
quite cheerfully. I believe Ben
is much better also and
that they like Carneoure.
I have paid no country
visits yet, but must soon,
if the weather improves. No letter from Emma or Jerry
this week. I suppose she is
staying on. I hear Emmie
Eggan is likely to go to the
Hills, it is thought too dangerous
to return. Colonel Buchanan
brought his wife and little
girl here last Sunday. She
was a Miss Harland and is
still very nice looking. She
looks much younger than he
does. Now. goodbye darling:
Much love Your ever loving
Mammy
